AlreadyExists.explanation: A file or folder already exists at `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`, so Cmdr can't create a new one there.
AlreadyExists.suggestion: Rename the existing item or choose a different name for the new one.
AlreadyExists.title: Already exists

Cancelled.explanation: The operation was cancelled before it could finish.
Cancelled.suggestion: Navigate here again whenever you're ready to retry.
Cancelled.title: Cancelled

ConnectionTimeout.explanation: Cmdr tried to access this resource but the connection didn't respond in time. This usually means the server or device is slow to respond, or the network connection is unstable.
ConnectionTimeout.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the device or server is powered on and reachable
- Check your Wi-Fi or Ethernet connection
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to test if the server is reachable
- Try again
ConnectionTimeout.title: Connection timed out

DeletePending.explanation: `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg` is on its way out. The server marked it for deletion, but another open handle is keeping it around until that handle closes.
DeletePending.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Wait a moment and try again — once the last handle closes, the file disappears
- Close any other apps that might have this file open
- If it sticks around, restart Cmdr to drop any handles it might still hold
DeletePending.title: File is being removed

DeviceDisconnected.explanation: The device holding `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg` was disconnected during the operation. This can happen if a USB cable comes loose, a phone goes to sleep, or a network drive drops its connection.
DeviceDisconnected.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Reconnect the device and make sure the cable is secure
- If it's a phone, unlock it and make sure file transfer mode is active
- Navigate here again once the device is back
DeviceDisconnected.title: Device disconnected

IoError:none.explanation: Cmdr ran into a problem with `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`: Protocol error: STATUS_DELETE_PENDING during Create. This could be a temporary glitch or a sign that the disk or device needs attention.
IoError:none.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the disk or device is still connected
- Try again
- If this keeps happening, try running **Disk Utility > First Aid** on this volume
IoError:none.title: Couldn't read this folder

IsADirectory.explanation: Cmdr tried to open `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg` as a file, but it's a folder.
IsADirectory.suggestion: Navigate into the folder instead of opening it as a file.
IsADirectory.title: This is a folder, not a file

NotFound.explanation: Cmdr couldn't find `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`. It may have been moved, renamed, or deleted while Cmdr was trying to access it.
NotFound.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the path is spelled correctly
- If this is on a network drive, make sure it's connected and the share is accessible
- Navigate to the parent folder and look for the item there
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on the parent folder to see what's there
NotFound.title: Path not found

NotSupported.explanation: This operation isn't supported on this type of volume. Some volumes (like phone storage or certain network drives) don't support all operations.
NotSupported.suggestion: Try a different approach, or use Finder for this operation.
NotSupported.title: Not supported

PermissionDenied:plain.explanation: Cmdr doesn't have permission to access `/some/plain/folder/_x_*y`. macOS controls which apps can access which folders, and Cmdr hasn't been granted access to this one yet.
PermissionDenied:plain.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Open **System Settings > Privacy & Security > Files & Folders** and grant Cmdr access
- Check the folder's permissions in Finder: right-click the folder, choose Get Info, and look under Sharing & Permissions
- If this is a shared folder, ask the owner to update permissions
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on the path to see the current permissions
PermissionDenied:plain.title: No permission

PermissionDenied:tcc.explanation: Cmdr doesn't have permission to access `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`. macOS controls which apps can access which folders, and Cmdr hasn't been granted access to this one yet.
PermissionDenied:tcc.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Open **System Settings > Privacy & Security > Files & Folders** and grant Cmdr access
- Check the folder's permissions in Finder: right-click the folder, choose Get Info, and look under Sharing & Permissions
- If this is a shared folder, ask the owner to update permissions
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on the path to see the current permissions
PermissionDenied:tcc.title: No permission

ReadOnly.explanation: This volume is read-only, so Cmdr can't make changes to it. This could be because the device has a physical write-protection switch, the disk image was mounted as read-only, or the file system doesn't support writing.
ReadOnly.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- If the device has a physical write-protection switch (common on SD cards), flip it off
- If this is a disk image, remount it with write access
- Otherwise, copy the files to a writable location first
ReadOnly.title: Read-only

StorageFull.explanation: There isn't enough free space on this volume to complete the operation.
StorageFull.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Free up space by moving or deleting files you no longer need
- Empty the Trash (right-click the Trash icon in the Dock)
- In Terminal, run `df -h` to see how much space is left on each volume
StorageFull.title: Disk is full

emptyRoot:icloud.explanation:
Cmdr opened iCloud Drive but it came back with no files. macOS hides iCloud Drive contents from apps that don't have **Full Disk Access**, so granting Cmdr that permission is the most likely fix.

If your iCloud Drive really is empty, you can ignore this hint.
emptyRoot:icloud.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Open [**System Settings > Privacy & Security**](x-apple.systempreferences:com.apple.preference.security?Privacy) and pick **Full Disk Access**
- Add Cmdr (use the **+** button) and toggle it on
- Quit and reopen Cmdr
- Come back here to retry
emptyRoot:icloud.title: iCloud Drive looks empty

errno:1.explanation: macOS blocked Cmdr from accessing `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`. This usually means the folder is protected by macOS security policies, or Cmdr hasn't been granted the right permissions yet.
errno:1.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Open **System Settings > Privacy & Security > Files & Folders** and grant Cmdr access
- If this is a system-protected folder (like system directories), you may need to grant Cmdr **Full Disk Access** under Privacy & Security
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on this path to check ownership and permissions
errno:1.title: Not permitted

errno:12.explanation: The system ran out of available memory (RAM) while reading this folder. This can happen when many apps are open at once, or when a folder contains a very large number of files.
errno:12.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Close some apps to free up memory, especially ones using lots of resources (browsers with many tabs, editors, media apps)
- Check memory usage in **Activity Monitor** (search for it in Spotlight)
- Navigate here again to retry
errno:12.title: Not enough memory

errno:13.explanation: Cmdr doesn't have permission to access `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`. macOS controls which apps can access which folders, and Cmdr hasn't been granted access to this one yet.
errno:13.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Open **System Settings > Privacy & Security > Files & Folders** and grant Cmdr access
- Check the folder's permissions in Finder: right-click it, choose Get Info, and look under Sharing & Permissions
- If this is a shared folder, ask the owner to update permissions
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on this path to see the current permissions
errno:13.title: No permission

errno:16.explanation: Cmdr couldn't access `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg` because another app or process is currently using it exclusively. This is usually temporary.
errno:16.suggestion: Wait a moment, then navigate here again. If it keeps happening, check which app might be holding the file open (in Terminal, run `lsof +D <folder-path>` to see which processes are using this folder).
errno:16.title: Resource busy

errno:17.explanation: A file or folder already exists at `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`, so Cmdr can't create a new one there.
errno:17.suggestion: Rename the existing item or choose a different name for the new one.
errno:17.title: Already exists

errno:18.explanation: Cmdr can't move this item directly because the source and destination are on different volumes (for example, an internal drive and a USB stick). Moving across volumes requires copying the data and then removing the original.
errno:18.suggestion: Copy the item to the destination instead of moving it. Cmdr will handle the copy automatically.
errno:18.title: Cross-device operation

errno:2.explanation: Cmdr couldn't find `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`. It may have been moved, renamed, or deleted while Cmdr was trying to access it.
errno:2.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the path is spelled correctly

- Navigate to the parent folder and look for the item there
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on the parent folder to see what's there
errno:2.title: Path not found

errno:20.explanation: Cmdr expected `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg` to be a folder, but it's a file. This can happen if something was recently renamed or replaced.
errno:20.suggestion: Check the path and make sure it points to a folder, not a file.
errno:20.title: Not a folder

errno:21.explanation: Cmdr expected `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg` to be a file, but it's a folder. This can happen if something was recently renamed or replaced.
errno:21.suggestion: Check the path and make sure it points to a file, not a folder.
errno:21.title: Is a folder

errno:22.explanation: The system returned an unexpected response for this operation. This can happen when a volume's file system has inconsistencies, or when the volume is in an unusual state.
errno:22.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Navigate here again to retry
- If this keeps happening, open **Disk Utility** (search for it in Spotlight) and run **First Aid** on this volume to check for file system problems
errno:22.title: Unexpected system response

errno:28.explanation: There isn't enough free space on this volume to complete the operation.
errno:28.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Free up space by moving or deleting files you no longer need
- Empty the Trash (right-click the Trash icon in the Dock)
- In Terminal, run `df -h` to see how much space is left on each volume
- Check **System Settings > General > Storage** for a breakdown of what's using space
errno:28.title: Disk is full

errno:30.explanation: This volume is mounted as read-only, so Cmdr can't make changes to it. This could be because the device has a physical write-protection switch, the disk image was mounted read-only, or the file system doesn't support writing.
errno:30.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- If the device has a physical write-protection switch (common on SD cards), flip it off
- If this is a disk image, remount it with write access
- Otherwise, copy the files to a writable location first
errno:30.title: Read-only volume

errno:35.explanation: The system is momentarily too busy to handle this request. This is a transient condition that typically clears up on its own within seconds.
errno:35.suggestion: Navigate here again to retry. This usually resolves on its own. If it keeps happening, the system might be under heavy load. Check **Activity Monitor** for apps consuming a lot of resources.
errno:35.title: Temporarily unavailable

errno:4.explanation: A system operation was interrupted before it could finish. This is almost always a one-off, caused by a signal or background process momentarily getting in the way.
errno:4.suggestion: Navigate here again to retry. This kind of interruption almost never happens twice in a row.
errno:4.title: Interrupted

errno:45.explanation: This operation isn't supported on this file system. Different file systems (like FAT32, NTFS, or network shares) support different features, and this one doesn't support what Cmdr is trying to do.
errno:45.suggestion: Try a different approach, or use Finder for this operation. If you're working with an external drive, it might be formatted with a file system that has limitations (for example, FAT32 can't store files larger than 4 GB).
errno:45.title: Not supported

errno:5.explanation: Cmdr hit a hardware-level read problem at `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`. This means the disk or device had trouble reading the data, which could be a temporary glitch or a sign of a failing disk.
errno:5.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the disk or device is still properly connected
- Open **Disk Utility** (search for it in Spotlight) and run **First Aid** on this volume
- If this keeps happening, back up your data as soon as possible. The disk may be developing bad sectors or starting to wear out.
errno:5.title: Disk read problem

errno:50.explanation: Your Mac's network connection is down, so Cmdr can't reach this volume. This could mean Wi-Fi is disconnected, an Ethernet cable is unplugged, or the network interface is disabled.
errno:50.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check Wi-Fi or Ethernet status in **System Settings > Network**
- If you're on Wi-Fi, try turning it off and on again
- In Terminal, run `ping google.com` to test your connection
- Navigate here again once you're back online
errno:50.title: Network is down

errno:51.explanation: Cmdr can't reach the network this volume is on. This often means you're not connected to the right network, or a VPN isn't active.
errno:51.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check your Wi-Fi or Ethernet connection
- Make sure you're on the right network (for example, your office Wi-Fi or VPN)
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to test if the server is reachable
- Navigate here again once you're connected
errno:51.title: Network unreachable

errno:52.explanation: The network connection was unexpectedly reset while Cmdr was reading this folder. This can happen when a router restarts, a VPN reconnects, or the network is temporarily unstable.
errno:52.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check your network connection
- If you're on a VPN, make sure it's still connected
- Navigate here again to retry
errno:52.title: Network connection dropped

errno:53.explanation: The connection was dropped by the server or the network before Cmdr could finish reading. This often means the server is overloaded or restarting.
errno:53.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the server is running and responsive
- Check your network connection
- Navigate here again to retry
errno:53.title: Connection dropped

errno:54.explanation: The remote server closed the connection unexpectedly. This can happen when the server restarts, hits a timeout, or runs into an internal problem.
errno:54.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the server is running
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to test if the server is reachable
- Navigate here again to retry
errno:54.title: Connection reset

errno:60.explanation: Cmdr tried to read this folder but the connection didn't respond in time. This usually means the server or device is slow, unreachable, or the network between you and it is congested.
errno:60.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the device or server is powered on and reachable
- Check your Wi-Fi or Ethernet connection
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to test connectivity
- Navigate here again to retry
errno:60.title: Connection timed out

errno:61.explanation: The server actively refused the connection. This usually means the server software (for example, an SMB or NFS service) isn't running, or it's configured to reject connections from this Mac.
errno:61.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the server is running and its file sharing service is active
- Verify the server address and port are correct
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to check if the server is reachable at all
- Navigate here again to retry
errno:61.title: Connection refused

errno:62.explanation: Cmdr found a circular chain of symbolic links (shortcuts that point to other shortcuts) at `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`. Following these links leads in a circle, so Cmdr can't reach the actual file or folder.
errno:62.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on this path to see where the symbolic links point
- Find and fix the link that creates the loop
- If you're not sure which link is the problem, follow them one by one with `readlink <path>`
errno:62.title: Symlink loop

errno:63.explanation: The file or folder name exceeds the system's limit (255 characters on most Mac volumes). This can also happen when the full path (all folders combined) exceeds the system's maximum path length.
errno:63.suggestion: Rename the item to use a shorter name. If the name looks reasonable, the full path (including all parent folders) might be too long. Try moving it to a shorter path.
errno:63.title: Name too long

errno:64.explanation: The remote host (the computer or server hosting this volume) isn't responding. It may be powered off, sleeping, or temporarily unreachable.
errno:64.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the host is powered on and connected to the network
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to test if it's reachable
- If it's a NAS or server, check its management interface
- Navigate here again once the host is back
errno:64.title: Host is down

errno:65.explanation: Cmdr can't find a network route to the host this volume is on. This usually means the host is on a different network, behind a firewall, or the routing configuration needs updating.
errno:65.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the host is powered on and on the same network
- If you need a VPN to reach it, make sure the VPN is connected
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to test connectivity
- Navigate here again once the host is reachable
errno:65.title: Host unreachable

errno:66.explanation: Cmdr can't remove `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg` because it still contains files or subfolders. The system requires a folder to be empty before it can be removed this way.
errno:66.suggestion: Delete the contents of the folder first, then try removing the folder again.
errno:66.title: Folder not empty

errno:69.explanation: You've reached your disk quota (the maximum amount of space allocated to your user account) on this volume. This is common on shared servers and network drives where an administrator sets per-user limits.
errno:69.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Free up space by removing files you no longer need on this volume
- Ask your system administrator to increase your quota
- In Terminal, run `quota` to see your current usage and limit
errno:69.title: Quota exceeded

errno:70.explanation: Cmdr is trying to access this folder using an old reference that the server no longer recognizes. This commonly happens with network drives (NFS, SMB) after the server restarts, the share is remounted, or the connection was interrupted.
errno:70.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Navigate away from this folder and come back
- If this is a network drive, try unmounting and remounting it in Finder
- Check that the server hosting this folder is running
- In Terminal, run `mount` to see currently mounted volumes
errno:70.title: Stale connection

errno:77.explanation: The system ran out of file locks. File locks are how apps coordinate access to shared files (preventing two apps from writing to the same file at once). Running out usually means too many apps are accessing files simultaneously.
errno:77.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Close some apps, especially ones that work with many files (editors, IDEs, backup tools)
- In Terminal, run `lsof | wc -l` to see how many files are open across all apps
- If the problem keeps happening, you can raise the limit with `ulimit -n 4096` in Terminal
- Navigate here again to retry
errno:77.title: Lock unavailable

errno:80.explanation: Cmdr couldn't authenticate with this volume. Your saved credentials may have expired, or the server is rejecting the current login.
errno:80.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Disconnect and reconnect the volume, and enter your username and password again
- Check that your password hasn't changed or expired
- If this is a company server, check with your IT team
errno:80.title: Authentication required

errno:81.explanation: This volume requires you to log in, but no credentials have been provided yet.
errno:81.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Disconnect and reconnect the volume in Finder
- Enter your username and password when prompted
- If you're not sure about the credentials, check with the server's administrator
errno:81.title: Authentication required

errno:82.explanation: The device is powered off or in a deep sleep state, so Cmdr can't communicate with it.
errno:82.suggestion: Turn on the device, wait for it to fully start up, then navigate here again.
errno:82.title: Device powered off

errno:83.explanation: The device reported a hardware-level problem. This could be a loose connection, a worn-out cable, or an issue with the device itself.
errno:83.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Disconnect and reconnect the device
- Try a different USB port or cable
- If it's an external drive, try connecting it to a different computer to see if the problem follows the device
- If this keeps happening, the device may need repair or replacement
errno:83.title: Device problem

errno:89.explanation: The operation was cancelled before it could finish.
errno:89.suggestion: Navigate here again whenever you're ready to retry.
errno:89.title: Cancelled

errno:93.explanation: Cmdr tried to read a file attribute (extra metadata like tags or permissions) that doesn't exist on this item. This can happen when the file system doesn't support extended attributes, or when the attribute was removed.
errno:93.suggestion: This file system may not support the metadata Cmdr needs. Try the operation on a different volume, or copy the file to your Mac's internal drive first.
errno:93.title: Attribute not found

errno:9999.explanation: Cmdr ran into an unexpected problem reading `/Volumes/share/_todo_*pics/photo.jpg`. Check the technical details below for the specific system code, which can help with troubleshooting.
errno:9999.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the disk or device is still connected
- Navigate here again to retry
- If this keeps happening, open **Disk Utility** and run **First Aid** on this volume
errno:9999.title: Couldn't read this folder

git:BareRepo.explanation: Bare repos don't have a working tree, and the git browser is built around one.
git:BareRepo.suggestion: Clone the repo into a working directory to use the git browser.
git:BareRepo.title: Bare repos aren't supported yet

git:BlobTooLarge.explanation: Cmdr reads git blobs whole-file at a time, and this one's over the safety cap.
git:BlobTooLarge.suggestion: Check out the file from a working tree if you want to read it.
git:BlobTooLarge.title: This file's too big to load from history

git:CorruptRepo.explanation: Some of the on-disk repo data is unreadable. The folder might have been edited outside git.
git:CorruptRepo.suggestion: Run `git fsck` to inspect the repo. A fresh clone often clears it up.
git:CorruptRepo.title: This repo looks damaged

git:GitDirPermissionDenied.explanation: The OS denied access to the `.git` folder, even though the working tree is readable.
git:GitDirPermissionDenied.suggestion: Open **System Settings > Privacy & Security > Files and Folders** and grant Cmdr access. In Terminal, `ls -la .git` shows the current owner and mode.
git:GitDirPermissionDenied.title: Cmdr can't open the `.git` folder

git:IndexLocked.explanation: Git's index is locked, which usually means another git command is still running.
git:IndexLocked.suggestion: Wait for the running git command to finish, then navigate here again.
git:IndexLocked.title: Another git is mid-write

git:MissingObject.explanation: Git is looking for an object that's no longer in the pack files. The repo might be partially fetched or damaged.
git:MissingObject.suggestion: Try `git fetch` to repopulate the missing object, or `git fsck` to inspect the damage.
git:MissingObject.title: A git object is missing

git:NotARepo.explanation: Cmdr looked up the folder tree and didn't find a `.git` here.
git:NotARepo.suggestion: Open a folder inside a git clone to see the repo chip.
git:NotARepo.title: No git repo here

git:OrphanedWorktree.explanation: This is a linked worktree but its main repo is missing, so git can't follow the link.
git:OrphanedWorktree.suggestion: Try opening the main repo, or remove the orphan with `git worktree prune`.
git:OrphanedWorktree.title: This worktree is orphaned

git:PermissionDenied.explanation: The OS won't let Cmdr open the `.git` folder, so git info isn't available.
git:PermissionDenied.suggestion: Open **System Settings > Privacy & Security > Files and Folders** and grant Cmdr access to the folder.
git:PermissionDenied.title: Cmdr can't read this repo

git:ShallowBoundary.explanation: This commit lives past the boundary of your shallow clone, so its data isn't on disk.
git:ShallowBoundary.suggestion: Run `git fetch --unshallow` (or `--depth=N`) to bring more history into the clone.
git:ShallowBoundary.title: Beyond the shallow-clone boundary
